title: Linux Cron and Job Scheduling
description: Schedule recurring and one-time tasks using crontab, at, and systemd timers for automated system maintenance.

tasks.prompt: List the current user's crontab entries.
tasks.explanation: 'crontab -l' displays all scheduled cron jobs for the invoking user.

tasks.prompt: Open the current user's crontab file in the default editor for editing.
tasks.explanation: 'crontab -e' opens the user's crontab in $EDITOR, allowing addition or modification of scheduled jobs.

tasks.prompt: Remove all crontab entries for the current user.
tasks.explanation: 'crontab -r' deletes the entire crontab for the current user. Use with caution in production.

tasks.prompt: List the crontab entries for user 'backup' (requires root).
tasks.hint: Use crontab with user flag (-u): 'sudo crontab -l -u backup'
tasks.explanation: 'crontab -u username' manages another user's crontab; root privileges are typically required.

tasks.prompt: Schedule a one-time job to run at 14:30 today using 'at' (interactive prompt).
tasks.explanation: 'at' schedules a command to run once at a specified time. You enter commands at the interactive prompt.

tasks.prompt: List all pending 'at' jobs for the current user.
tasks.hint: Use atq to list pending jobs: 'atq'
tasks.explanation: 'atq' (at queue) shows scheduled one-time jobs waiting to execute.

tasks.prompt: List all active systemd timers and their next scheduled run times.
tasks.explanation: systemd timers are a modern alternative to cron. 'list-timers' shows when each timer will fire next.

tasks.prompt: Display the anacron execution timestamps from '/var/spool/anacron/cron.daily'.
tasks.hint: Use cat to view the file: 'cat /var/spool/anacron/cron.daily'
tasks.explanation: Anacron runs jobs that may have been missed during downtime. Its spool files record last execution dates.
